sound_type: high melodious baying flock -- polyphonic chorus of dozens to hundreds of swans calling simultaneously, 500-3000 Hz, whistling quavering quality, flock-driven E=5, audible at 500-1500 meters over delta marshes, former 'Whistling Swan'
ecosystem: Delta marsh, winter visitor. Breeds on Arctic tundra (low Arctic from Alaska to northern Canada). Winters in PNW lowlands, especially Skagit Valley. Agricultural fields, shallow wetlands, estuarine habitats.
season: Winter visitor to PNW. Arrives October-November, departs March-April. 4-5 month winter residency. Baying most prominent during fall arrival, spring departure, and winter feeding gatherings.
family: Anatidae (10th consecutive: 118-127 -- NEW ALL-TIME RECORD, breaks Picidae nonet)

coordinates.description: Reference for Skagit Valley wintering habitat. Fir Island area between north and south forks of the Skagit River. One of the most reliable Tundra Swan viewing locations in the PNW.

taxonomy_note.description: TENTH consecutive Anatidae (118-127). BREAKS the Picidae nonet record (9 consecutive, 107-115). New all-time family streak. First swan in the engine. Comparison pair with Trumpeter Swan (degree 128). North American subspecies C. c. columbianus formerly called Whistling Swan.
taxonomy_note.current_family: Anatidae (10th consecutive -- NEW ALL-TIME RECORD, streak ongoing)
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Anatidae count: extensive. Ten consecutive at degrees 118-127, plus earlier entries. By far the most represented family.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Cygnus from Latin/Greek for 'swan.' columbianus after the Columbia River region where Lewis and Clark first collected the species. Former common name 'Whistling Swan' reflects the high, whistling call quality.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Cygnus -- first entry. Two North American species: Tundra Swan (127) and Trumpeter Swan (128). Both documented in consecutive degrees.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Anseriformes. Tenth consecutive Anatidae. The order's family streak continues to new heights.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Anatidae at tenth consecutive. RECORD BROKEN. Trumpeter Swan at 128 will extend to eleven. The streak shows no sign of ending.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 500-3,000 Hz. Higher than Trumpeter Swan (200-1,500 Hz). The whistling, quavering quality sits in mid-to-upper frequency range.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 800-2,000 Hz. Peak baying energy in the mid-frequency band where the melodious quality is strongest.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Individual call: high-pitched whoo-hoo or wow-wow-wow at 500-3,000 Hz. Flock baying: polyphonic overlay of dozens-hundreds of individual calls, creating shimmering harmonic texture.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual call: 0.5-2 seconds. Flock baying: continuous, sustained for minutes to hours during active feeding or flight. A winter morning on the Skagit Flats: baying may persist as long as the flock is active.
song_metrics.note_structure: MELODIOUS BAYING: High-pitched whoo-hoo or wow-wow-wow. Quavering, rising-falling pitch. Musical quality. Former name Whistling Swan reflects the tonal character. Individual notes are soft; flock produces E=5 through collective amplification.
song_metrics.note_types: Baying call (primary, high melodious), flight contact call (similar but shorter), alarm call (sharper, higher), juvenile begging call (higher, thinner). The baying is the defining sound.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Irregular. Individual calls overlap in a flock, creating continuous polyphonic texture. No coordinated phrase structure -- each bird calls independently. The beauty is in the uncoordinated overlap.
song_metrics.timbre: Melodious. Whistling. Quavering. Musical. High-pitched compared to Trumpeter Swan's deep bugling. The former name 'Whistling Swan' captures the tonal quality precisely.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5. Individual call: moderate (E=3-4). Flock baying: moderate-high (E=5). The flock multiplier is the key: 50-200 swans calling simultaneously produces landscape-filling sound. Audible at 500-1,500 meters over open delta terrain.
song_metrics.tempo: Irregular. Individual calls at 2-5 second intervals. In a flock, the overlapping calls create near-continuous sound. Tempo varies with activity: higher during flight, lower during feeding.
song_metrics.context: Winter flocking -- feeding gatherings on agricultural fields and delta marshes. Flight calls during transit between feeding and roosting areas. Arrival and departure (fall/spring) feature intense calling. The baying announces the swans' presence across the winter landscape.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal vocal variation across range. The call is largely innate. However, flock size varies regionally, affecting the collective volume. Skagit Valley flocks are among the largest in the Pacific flyway.
song_metrics.key_property: HIGH MELODIOUS BAYING FLOCK -- polyphonic chorus of Cygnus columbianus. E=5. 500-3,000 Hz. Former 'Whistling Swan.' Flock-driven energy: individual call moderate, collective baying impressive. Audible 500-1,500m. TENTH CONSECUTIVE ANATIDAE (118-127) -- NEW ALL-TIME RECORD. First swan in the engine. Comparison pair with Trumpeter Swan (128): high baying (E=5) vs. deep bugling (E=6). Arctic breeder, PNW winter visitor. Skagit Valley primary wintering ground.

plumage.overall: Adult: entirely white plumage. Gray-pink bill with small yellow loral spot (present in most individuals). Legs and feet black. Juvenile: gray-brown plumage, pinkish bill. Family groups visible in winter flocks -- white adults with gray-brown young.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: YELLOW LORAL SPOT -- small patch of yellow skin at base of upper bill, in front of eye. Present in most Tundra Swans, absent in Trumpeter Swans. Concave (dished) bill profile vs. Trumpeter's straight profile. Rounder head shape. Smaller overall size.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Adults white year-round. Juveniles gray-brown in first winter, gradually molting to white. Second-year birds may show residual gray feathers.
plumage.flight_pattern: Long neck extended. Slow, powerful wingbeats. V-formation in flocks. Baying calls continuous during flight. Family groups maintain proximity.

physical_description.overall: Large white swan. Body length 120-150 cm (47-59 inches). Wingspan 168-211 cm (66-83 inches). Weight 3.4-9.6 kg (7.5-21 lbs). Smaller than Trumpeter Swan but still one of North America's largest birds.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Yellow loral spot (most individuals). Concave bill profile. Rounder head shape. Smaller than Trumpeter. High-pitched call (vs. Trumpeter's deep bugle). In mixed flocks, size and voice are the most reliable separators.
physical_description.field_id: Listen for the high, melodious baying -- higher-pitched and softer than Trumpeter's deep bugling. Look for the yellow loral spot at the bill base. Note the concave bill profile and rounder head shape. In the Skagit Valley, direct comparison with Trumpeters is often possible in mixed flocks.

nodes.title: 10th Consecutive Anatidae -- NEW ALL-TIME RECORD
nodes.description: Ten consecutive Anatidae (118-127). Breaks Picidae nonet record (9 consecutive, 107-115). From Harlequin Duck to Tundra Swan. Ducks to swans. Energy bookends: E=5 at start (118) and record-breaker (127).

nodes.title: High Melodious Baying -- Flock-Driven E=5
nodes.description: Polyphonic chorus from dozens-hundreds of swans. 500-3,000 Hz. Former 'Whistling Swan.' Individual call moderate; flock multiplier produces E=5. Audible 500-1,500m over delta marshes. One of the most evocative PNW winter sounds.

nodes.title: Swan Comparison Pair (127-128) -- Tundra vs. Trumpeter
nodes.description: Tundra (high baying, E=5, flock-driven, yellow loral spot, smaller) vs. Trumpeter (deep bugling, E=6, individual power, no spot, largest native bird). Two acoustic strategies within Cygnus. Mirrors Hairy/Downy WP (110-111) and Barrow's/Common Goldeneye (123-124).

nodes.title: Arctic-to-PNW Migration -- 3,000-4,000 km
nodes.description: Breeds on Arctic tundra. Migrates 3,000-4,000 km to PNW. Skagit Valley primary wintering ground. 80,000-100,000 Pacific population. Family groups maintain bonds. Winter residency October-April.

nodes.title: Skagit Valley Wintering Grounds
nodes.description: Skagit and Samish Flats, Padilla Bay, Fir Island. Winter aggregations 500-2,000+ swans. Agricultural fields and shallow wetlands. The baying fills the delta landscape on winter mornings.

cross_references.relationship: SWAN COMPARISON PAIR! Tundra (127, high baying, E=5, flock-driven, smaller) vs. Trumpeter (128, deep bugling, E=6, individual power, largest native bird). Two acoustic strategies within Cygnus. Consecutive degrees for the swan pair.
ENERGY BOOKEND! Harlequin (118, E=5) started the Anatidae streak. Tundra Swan (127, E=5) breaks the Picidae record at the same energy. The streak opens and achieves its record at matching energy.
STREAK CONTINUITY! Canvasback (126, E=4) to Tundra Swan (127, E=5). The energy rises from moderate to high at the record-breaking degree.
RECORD BROKEN! Picidae held 9 consecutive (107-115: flickers, sapsuckers, woodpeckers). Anatidae now at 10 (118-127). New all-time family streak.

NAMING PARALLEL! Common Goldeneye = 'the whistler' (wing-whistle, mechanical). Tundra Swan = 'Whistling Swan' (vocal whistle). Two 'whistlers' in the same Anatidae streak, different mechanisms: feather vs. syrinx.
